expected_scale: 200 req/s peak across all 3 HTTP endpoints combined; 50 msg/s sustained on each of task-events/task-reminders; p99 < 300ms REST, informal 5s p99 message-processing expectation; 3x growth expected within 12 months (docs-good/technical-vision.md, 'Scale & Extensibility Targets')
classification_evidence: TaskResource.java exposes live REST endpoints (@Path("/api/v1/tasks")) with three real methods; TaskEventConsumer/TaskReminderConsumer are wired Kafka listeners (@Incoming, backed by real connector/topic/failure-strategy/concurrency config in application.properties); TaskCleanupJob is a live @Scheduled(every="1h") job. Three concurrently-running entry-point types is the shape of an operating service, not a library (no published-artifact API surface), a CLI tool (no main/bin entry point), or a batch-job-only system (this has a synchronous HTTP surface in addition to the scheduled job).

docs-good/technical-vision.md's 'Scale & Extensibility Targets' section states concrete production figures (200 req/s peak, 50 msg/s sustained per channel, p99 latency budgets, 3x growth in 12 months tied to a feature 'rolling out to all users, not just the current beta cohort') -- a vision doc written for a system with live, growing usage, not a prototype or spike. No WIP/experimental/spike/POC language appears anywhere in docs-good.

docs-good/technical-vision.md's Versioning & Deprecation Policy (already-adopted /api/v1/ prefix, a formal channel-retirement process referencing 'zero producers/consumers') is the kind of policy a team writes once real external callers exist or are anticipated, reinforcing production-service over prototype.

No Dockerfile, Kubernetes manifest, or other explicit deployment descriptor exists anywhere in this repo (verified: find for *.yml/*.yaml/Dockerfile*/*k8s* returned nothing), so deployment_model ('multi-instance-service') is inferred from the REST+Kafka-consumer shape and per-consumer 'concurrency'/'enabled' config in application.properties rather than directly confirmed by a manifest -- flagged as an inference, not a hard-evidenced fact.

data_sensitivity classified 'internal-only' rather than 'pii': the only person-linked field in the schema (sql/001-task.sql, Task.java) is assigneeId, an opaque id column, not a name/email/contact field. No monetary/financial column exists anywhere in the one-table schema.

build.gradle states this repo is 'Illustrative only ... not a buildable project' and the top-level examples/sample-service/README.md frames it as a test fixture for this very skill. Per references/system-classification.md's explicit guidance ('Illustrative/non-buildable teaching fixtures still classify by architectural shape, not by disclaimer'), that meta-fact about the repo's purpose does not change what the in-scope artifacts (code + docs-good) present themselves as architecturally -- three live entry-point types plus a stated production-scale target is production-service shape regardless of buildability, so classification proceeds evidence-first on the architecture itself. confidence is 'high' because docs-good goes out of its way to state real-looking production targets rather than leaving them unstated, and because the code's entry points are unambiguous (REST + 2 Kafka consumers + scheduled job), not because the repo is actually deployed anywhere.

This run also classifies against the newly-added reliability-resilience/change-safety/security-boundaries dimensions (scripts/rubric_manifest.json, first run exercising them) -- all three stay mandatory under system_type_overrides' production-service entry (explicitly {} = nothing relaxed), consistent with treating this as a strict production-service audit.
